claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You do not live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 2 children:
  - Alex is 11 years old and does not live with you.
  - Blake is 15 years old and does not live with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant does not live in the UK
  - Child is 11 (under 16)
  - Child does not live with claimant and weekly upkeep (£10.00) is below the Child Benefit rate (£26.05)
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Regarding Blake:
  - Claimant does not live in the UK
  - Child is 15 (under 16)
  - Someone who lives with the child is already claiming
  - Child in care for 12 weeks but spends 24+ hours/week at home
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant does not live in the UK; Child does not live with claimant and weekly upkeep (£10.00) is below the Child Benefit rate (£26.05)
circumstances: Claimant does not live in the UK
Child does not live with claimant and weekly upkeep (£10.00) is below the Child Benefit rate (£26.05)
Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Hello! I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. To assess your eligibility, I'll need to ask you some questions about your circumstances.

Let me start the assessment process.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for reaching out! To help determine your eligibility for Child Benefit, I need to ask you a few questions about your situation.

**First question: Do you have a child (or children) that you're inquiring about?** If yes, could you tell me how old they are?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hello! Yes, I have two children I'd like to ask about. I have Alex who is 11, and Blake who is 15.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information! You have two children:
- Alex, who is 11 years old
- Blake, who is 15 years old

Both children are under 16, which is a good start for eligibility.

**Next question: Are either Alex or Blake working full-time (which means 24 hours or more per week)?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, neither of them work full-time. They're just kids at school.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Perfect, thank you. Since they're in school and not working full-time, that's good.

**Next question: Are either Alex or Blake receiving any benefits in their own right, such as Employment and Support Allowance or Universal Credit?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, neither of them get any benefits in their own name. They're just children.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Excellent. Since Alex is 11 and Blake is 15, and they're both in school, they meet the child-related criteria so far.

Now I need to ask about your circumstances:

**Do you normally live in the UK?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, I don't live in the UK. I'm currently living abroad.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for letting me know. Since you're living abroad, there are some special circumstances that might still make you eligible.

**Could you tell me more about your situation? Specifically:**

1. **Are you a Crown servant (such as a diplomat or member of HM Forces) working abroad?**
2. **Or do you have settled or pre-settled status under the EU Settlement Scheme?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, I'm neither a Crown servant nor do I have any settled or pre-settled status under the EU Settlement Scheme.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for providing that information. I need to clarify one more thing about your residency situation.

**Are you living in any of the following places that might have special arrangements with the UK:**
- **Gibraltar**
- **A country covered by a reciprocal agreement with the UK** (such as certain EEA countries or Switzerland under specific circumstances)

Or is there any other special connection to the UK that relates to your residency (for example, are you temporarily abroad but still considered normally resident in the UK)?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, none of those apply to me. I simply live abroad with no special arrangements or connections like that.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for clarifying your situation. Based on the information you've provided, I now have enough details to make an assessment.

Let me review what you've told me:

**About Alex (age 11):**
- Under 16 years old ✓
- Not working full-time ✓
- Not receiving benefits in their own right ✓
- Not in a disqualifying apprenticeship ✓

**About Blake (age 15):**
- Under 16 years old ✓
- Not working full-time ✓
- Not receiving benefits in their own right ✓
- Not in a disqualifying apprenticeship ✓

**About your circumstances:**
- You do not normally live in the UK ✗
- You are not a Crown servant abroad ✗
- You do not have settled or pre-settled status under the EU Settlement Scheme ✗
- You do not live in Gibraltar or a country with a reciprocal agreement ✗

Unfortunately, **you would not be eligible for Child Benefit** for either Alex or Blake. This is because one of the key requirements for Child Benefit is that the claimant must meet UK residency requirements. According to the eligibility rules, you must normally live in the UK or meet one of the special circumstances (such as being a Crown servant abroad or having certain settled status).

While both of your children would otherwise qualify based on their age and circumstances, your residency situation means you do not meet the claimant eligibility requirements for Child Benefit.

Is there anything else you'd like to know about Child Benefit or your situation?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Oh, I see. That's disappointing, but I understand. Just to be clear - even though the children themselves might be in the UK, because I'm not living there and don't have any of those special circumstances, I can't claim for them?

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
That's a very good question, and I should have asked you about this earlier!

**Are Alex and Blake currently living in the UK?** And if so, are they living with someone else who is responsible for them (such as another parent, a family member, or a guardian)?

This is important because Child Benefit can potentially be claimed by the person who is actually responsible for the child on a day-to-day basis, which might be someone other than yourself if the children are living in the UK with another person.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, both Alex and Blake are living in the UK, but not with me. They live with someone else who looks after them.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for clarifying that - this is very important information!

In this situation, Child Benefit eligibility would depend on the person who is actually responsible for the children in the UK. The person who has Alex and Blake living with them and is looking after them day-to-day may be eligible to claim Child Benefit instead.

**To clarify your own situation:** Since you don't live in the UK and don't meet any of the special residency circumstances, you would not be eligible to claim Child Benefit for Alex and Blake, even though they are living in the UK.

**However, the person who is currently looking after Alex and Blake in the UK might be eligible** if they:
- Are responsible for the children
- Meet the UK residency requirements (which they likely do if they live in the UK)
- Meet the other eligibility criteria

If that person wants to know if they're eligible, they would need to go through their own assessment based on their circumstances.

**For your specific situation:** You are not eligible for Child Benefit for Alex or Blake because you do not meet the UK residency requirements.

Is there anything else you'd like to understand about this?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: I see. So just to confirm - even if I'm helping to support them financially from abroad, because I don't live in the UK myself, I can't claim Child Benefit for them. Is that right?

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Yes, that's correct. Even if you are supporting Alex and Blake financially from abroad, you cannot claim Child Benefit for them because you do not meet the UK residency requirements.

Child Benefit is based on two key factors:
1. **The child must qualify** (which Alex and Blake both do based on their ages)
2. **The claimant (the person claiming) must meet the eligibility criteria**, which includes:
   - Meeting UK residency requirements, AND
   - Being responsible for the child

Since you don't normally live in the UK and don't have any of the special circumstances that would allow you to claim while abroad (such as being a Crown servant or having settled/pre-settled status), you are not eligible to claim Child Benefit, regardless of whether you're providing financial support.

The person who could potentially claim Child Benefit would be the person in the UK who has Alex and Blake living with them and is responsible for their day-to-day care.

I hope this clarifies the situation for you. Is there anything else I can help you with?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, that makes sense now. Thank you for explaining it to me. I understand that I'm not eligible because of where I live.
